content:
A silicon $P-N$ junction is shown in the figure. The doping in the $P$ region is $5 \times 10^{16} \mathrm{~cm}^3$ and doping in the $N$ region is $10 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$. The parameters given are

Built-in voltage $\left(\phi_{b i}\right)=0.8 \mathrm{~V}$

Electro charge $(q)=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$

Vacuum permittivity of silicon $\left(\varepsilon_{s i}\right)=12$

The magnitude of reverse bias voltage that would completely deplete one of the two regions ( $P$ or $N$ ) prior to the other (rounded off to one decimal place) is $\_\_\_\_$ V.
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { From question and the mentioned figure we have }\\ &\begin{aligned} & N_A=5 \times 10^{16} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3} \text { and } N_D=10 \times 10^{16} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3} \\ & V_{b i}=0.8 \mathrm{~V} . \\ & \varepsilon_{s i}=11.7 \\ & V_R=? \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Now we have $W=\sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon s}{q}\left[\frac{1}{N_A}+\frac{1}{N_D}\right]\left[V_{b i}+V_R\right]}$

or, $\quad \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 11.7 \times 8.852 \times 10^{-14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}}\left[0.8+V_R\right]$

or, $\quad W=\sqrt{12.46 \times 10^{+6} \times 10^{-16}[0.2+0.1]\left[0.8+V_R\right]}$

or, $\quad\left(W_n+W_p\right)=\sqrt{3.738 \times 10^{-10}\left[0.8+V_R\right]}$

Then $\quad N_D \cdot W_n=N_A \cdot W_p$

 $$ \begin{aligned}or,  W_p & =\left(\frac{N_D}{N_A}\right) \cdot W_n \\ & =0.4 \mu \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned} $$

So we have $\quad W=W n+W p=0.6 \mu \mathrm{~m}$

or, $\quad 0.6 \times 10^{-4}=\sqrt{3.738 \times\left[0.8+V_R\right]} \times 10^{-5}$

or, $\quad(6)^2=3.738\left[0.8+V_R\right]$

or, $\quad 36=3.738\left[0.8+V_R\right]$

or, $\quad 9.63=0.8+V_R$

or, $\quad V_R=8.83 \mathrm{~V}$

Hence, the correct answer is $(8.8$ to 9.0$)$.

content: Consider the recombination process via bulk traps in a forward biased $p n$ homojunction diode. The maximum recombination rate is $U_{\max }$. If the electron and the hole capture cross sections are equal, which one of the following is FALSE?

options.content: $U_{\max }$ occurs at the edges of the depletion region in the device.
$U_{\max }$ depends exponentially on the applied bias.
With all other parameters unchanged, $U_{\max }$ increases if the thermal velocity of carrier increases.
With all other parameters unchanged, $U_{\max }$ decreases if the intrinsic carrier density is reduced.

explanation:
The maximum recombination rate is given by,

$$ U_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} V_{t h} n_i \sigma_0 e^{V / 2 V_T} \cdot N_t $$

Where, $n_i=$ Intrinsic concentration, $\sigma_0=$ capture cross section, $V=$ applied voltage,

$V_T=$ thermal voltage, $V_{t h}=$ thermal velocity, $N_t=$ recombination centers in bulk of semiconductors

$$ \sigma_n=\sigma_p=\sigma_0 $$

In Homojunction $p-n$ diode we find that current in a pn junction can only exist if there is recombination or generation of electron and holes somewhere throughout the structure. The ideal diode equation is a result of the recombination and generation in the quasi-neutral regions (including recombination at the contacts) whereas recombination and generation in the depletion region yield enhanced leakage or photocurrents.

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content: An ideal p-n junction germanium diode has a reverse saturation current of $10 \mu \mathrm{~A}$ at 300 K . The voltage (in Volts, rounded off to two decimal places) to be applied across the junction to get a forward bias current of 100 mA at 300 K is __________. (Consider the Boltzmann constant $k_B=1.38 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$ and the charge of an electron $e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$.)
explanation:
To determine the voltage required across a p-n junction germanium diode to achieve a forward bias current of 100 mA at 300 K, we follow these steps:

Given Parameters:

Reverse saturation current, $ I_0 $: $ 10 \, \mu \mathrm{A} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{A} $

Temperature, $ T $: $ 300 \, \mathrm{K} $

Forward bias current, $ I_f $: $ 100 \, \mathrm{mA} = 100 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{A} $

Boltzmann constant, $ k_B $: $ 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \, \mathrm{J/K} $

Charge of an electron, $ e $: $ 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \, \mathrm{C} $

Formula for Forward Bias Current:

The forward bias current $ I_f $ is given by the formula:

$ I_f = I_0 \left( e^{V / V_T} - 1 \right) $

where $ V_T $ is the thermal voltage and is calculated as:

$ V_T = \frac{k_B T}{e} = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 25.86 \, \mathrm{mV} $

Calculation:

Given the equation

$ 100 \times 10^{-3} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \left( e^{V / 25.86 \times 10^{-3}} - 1 \right) $

Simplify to solve for $ V $:

$ 10^4 = e^{V / 25.86 \times 10^{-3}} $

$ 9999 = e^{V / 25.86 \times 10^{-3}} $

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides gives:

$ V = 25.86 \times 10^{-3} \, \ln(9999) $

Calculating the value:

$ V = 0.23 \, \mathrm{V} $

Hence, the voltage to be applied across the junction to achieve the desired forward current is 0.23 Volts.

content: The photocurrent of a PN junction diode solar cell is 1 mA. The voltage corresponding to its maximum power point is 0.3 V. If the thermal voltage is 30 mV, the reverse saturation current of the diode (in nA, rounded off to two decimal places) is _____.
explanation:
Given : Photo current $\left(I_{p h}\right)=1 \mathrm{~mA}$

Voltage corresponding to maximum power point $(V)=0.3 \mathrm{~V}$

Thermal voltage $\left(V_T\right)=30 \mathrm{mV}$

For solar cell

$$ \begin{aligned} & I=I_D-I_{p h} \\ & I=I_0 e^{V_D / \eta V_T}-I_{p h} \quad\left[\because I_D=I_0 e^{V_D / \eta V_T}\right] \end{aligned} $$

Power $(P)=V I$

Power $(P)=V\left[I_0 e^{V_D / \eta V_T}-I_{p h}\right] \quad\left[\because V_D=V\right]$

Power $(P)=V\left[I_0 e^{V / V_T}-I_{p h}\right] \quad[\because \eta=1]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$

Given when $V=0.3 \mathrm{~V}$ at that time power is maximum.

For maximum power, $\frac{d P}{d V}=0$

From equation (i),

Power $(P)=V\left[I_0 e^{V / V_T}-I_{p h}\right]$

$$ \begin{aligned} & P=V I_0 e^{V / V_T}-I_{p h} V \\ & \frac{d P}{d V}=I_0\left[\frac{V e^{V / V_T}}{V_T}+e^{V / V_T}\right]-I_{p h} \\ & \frac{d P}{d V}=I_0\left[\frac{V e^{V / V_T}}{V_T}+e^{V / V_T}\right]-I_{p h} \\ & \frac{d P}{d V}=I_0\left[\frac{V e^{V / V_T}}{V_T}+e^{V / V_T}\right]-I_{p h} \\ & I_0\left[\frac{V e^{V / V_T}}{V_T}+e^{V / V_T}\right]=I_{p h} \\ & e^{V / V_T}\left[\frac{V}{V_T}+1\right]=\frac{I_{p h t}}{I_0} \\ & I_0=\frac{I_{p h t}}{e^{V / V_r}\left[\frac{V}{V_T}+1\right]}=\frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{e^{0.3 / 0.03}\left[\frac{0.3}{0.03}+1\right]} \\ & I_0=4.127 \mathrm{nA}=4.13 \mathrm{nA} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the reverse saturation current of diode is 4.13 nA .

content: A p-type semiconductor with zero electric field is under illumination (low level injection) in steady state condition. Excess minority carrier density is zero at x = $$\pm$$ 2ln, where ln = 10$$-$$4 cm is the diffusion length of electrons. Assume electronic charge, q = $$-$$1.6 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$19 C. The profiles of photo-generation rate of carriers and the recombination rate of excess minority carriers (R) are shown. Under these conditions, the magnitude of the current density due to the photo-generated electrons at x = +2ln is ___________ mA/cm2 (rounded off to two decimal places).
explanation:
$$\delta n(x) = R{\tau _n} = {10^{20}}{e^{ - |x|/{l_n}}}{\tau _n}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\delta n(\ln ) = 1020\,{e^{ - 1}}{\tau _n}$$ ..... (i)

$${{\mathop{\rm l}\nolimits} _n} \le x \le 2{l_n}$$

Continuity equation in steady state,

$${D_n}{{{\partial ^2}\delta n} \over {\delta {x^2}}} + G - R = 0$$

Since, $$\left. \matrix{ G = 0 \hfill \cr R = 0 \hfill \cr} \right\}{l_n} \le x \le 2{l_n}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${D_n}{{{\partial ^2}\delta n} \over {\delta {x^2}}} = 0$$

Whose solution is, $$\delta n(x) = Ax + B$$

Since, at $$x = 2{l_n}$$ : $$\delta n(2{l_n}) = 0$$ (given)

$$0 = A(2{l_n}) + B$$

$$A = - {B \over {2{l_n}}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\delta n(x) = - {B \over {2{l_n}}}x + B = B\left( {1 - {x \over {2{l_n}}}} \right)$$ ...... (ii)

$$\therefore$$ At x = ln : equation (i) = equation (ii)

$${10^{20}}{e^{ - 1}}{\tau _n} = B\left( {1 - {{{l_n}} \over {2{l_n}}}} \right)$$

$$\therefore$$ $$B = 2 \times {10^{20}}{e^{ - 1}}{\tau _n}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\delta n(x) = 2 \times {10^{20}}{e^{ - 1}}{\tau _n}\left( {1 - {x \over {2{l_n}}}} \right)$$

$${l_n} \le x \le 2{l_n}$$

$$\therefore$$ Electron diffusion current density :

$${\left| {{J_n}} \right|_{diff}} = q{D_n}{{dn} \over {dx}} = q{D_n} \times 2 \times {10^{20}} \times {e^{ - 1}} \times {\tau _n}\left( {0 - {1 \over {2{l_n}}}} \right)$$

$$ = {{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times l_n^2 \times 2 \times {{10}^{20}} \times {e^{ - 1}}} \over {2{l_n}}}$$

$$ = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}} \times {l_n} \times {10^{20}} \times {e^{ - 1}}$$

$$ = 1.6 \times {10^1} \times 1 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times {e^{ - 1}}$$ ($${l_n} = {10^{ - 4}}$$ cm)

= 0.588 mA/cm2

= 0.59

content: A $p n$ junction solar cell of area $1.0 \mathrm{~cm}^2$, illuminated uniformly with $100 \mathrm{mWcm}^{-2}$; has the following parameter : Efficiency $=15 \%$, open circuit voltage $=0.7 \mathrm{~V}$, fill factor $=0.8$, and thickness $=200 \mu \mathrm{~m}$. The charge of an electron is $1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$. The average optical generation rate ( $\mathrm{in} \mathrm{cm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ ) is
explanation:
Given : Solar cell of area $=1.0 \mathrm{~cm}^2$, Input power $P_{\mathrm{opt}}=100 \mathrm{mWcm}^{-2}$,

Efficiency $\eta=15 \%=0.15$, Open circuit voltage $V_{o c}=0.7 \mathrm{~V}$, Fill factor $\mathrm{FF}=0.8$, and thickness $t=200 \mu \mathrm{~m}$.

Change of electron, $q=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$

Area $A=1.0 \mathrm{~cm}^2$

We know that, $\eta=\frac{\left(P_{o u t}\right)_{\max }}{P_{o p t}}=\frac{F F \times V_{O C} \times I_{S C}}{P_{o p t}}$

Also, given $P_{o p t}=100 \mathrm{~mW} / \mathrm{cm}^2$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 0.15=\frac{0.8 \times 0.7 \times I_{S C}}{100 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & I_{S C}=26.78 \mathrm{~mA} \end{array} $$

$\therefore$ Optical generation rate $G$ in $\mathrm{cm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ is,

$$ \begin{aligned} G & =\frac{I_{S C}}{q \times \text { Volume }}=\frac{I_{S C}}{q \times A \times t} \\ G & =\frac{26.78 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1 \times 200 \times 10^{-4}} \\ G & =\frac{1.67 \times 10^{19}}{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\ G & =0.835 \times 10^{19}=0.84 \times 10^{19} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content: A one-sided abrupt $p n$ junction diode has a depletion capacitance $C_D$ of 50 pF at a reverse bias 0.2 V . The plot of $\frac{1}{C_D^2}$ versus the applied voltage $V$ for this diode is a straight line as shown in the figure below. The slope of the plot is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{20} \mathrm{~F}^{-2} \mathrm{~V}^{-1}$.
explanation:
$$ \text { Given : Plot of } \frac{1}{C_D^2} \text { versus reverse bias voltage is shown in figure. } $$

For abrupt $p n$ junction,

$$ C_D \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{V_j}} $$

Where,  $$ V_j=V_R+V_{D_i} \quad V_R \approx V \quad \text { (in question) } $$

$$ C_D=\frac{K}{\sqrt{V+V_{b_i}}} $$

Slope $=\frac{1}{K^2} \quad($ Coming positive as we suppose $V$ to be increasing in negative direction $)$

It is clear from the given plot that $V_{b i} \neq 0$ as intersection with $y$-axis is at other than origin. So we cannot assume $V_{b i}$ to be zero.

Intersection point $=\frac{1}{K^2} \cdot V_{b i}$

Actual slope $=-\frac{1}{K^2}$

Given at $V=-0.2 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{C}_D=50 \mathrm{pF}, \frac{1}{C_D^2}=\frac{1}{2500 \times 10^{-24}}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2500 \times 10^{-24}}=\frac{-0.2}{K^2}+\frac{V_{b i}}{K^2} \\ & \frac{1}{K^2}\left(V_{b i}-0.2\right)=\frac{1 \times 10^{24}}{2500} \\ & \frac{1}{K^2}=\frac{10000 \times 10^{20}}{2500\left(V_{b i}-0.2\right)} \mathrm{F}^{-1} \mathrm{~V}^{-1} \\ & \frac{1}{K^2}=\frac{4}{\left(V_{b i}-0.2\right)} \times 10^{20} \mathrm{~F}^{-2} \mathrm{~V}^{-1} \\ & \left(\frac{1}{K^2}\right)_{\min }=\frac{4}{\left(V_{b i_{\max }}-0.2\right)} \times 10^{20} \end{aligned} $$

As $\left|V_{b_i}-0.2\right|$ is always less than 1 , so $\left(\frac{4}{V_{b i}-0.2}\right)$ will always greater than 4

$$ \therefore \quad \mid \text { Slope }\left|=\left|\frac{1}{K^2}\right|>4 \times 10^{20}\right. $$

The only option that satisfies the value of slope is -5.7 .

Hence, option (B) can be selected.

IIT has declared it as MTA.

In the first answer key, IIT has given its answer to be option (B), which is correct but is based on assumptions, resulting in making this question to be a wrong options eliminator type question.

The value of slope is never going to be exactly -5.7 according to the available data but it must be predicted that the magnitude of slope will be greater than four.

So, for the question to be valid, there must be statements given as the options to identify true or false possible value of slope, but not the exact value is possible to predict from the given data.

Hence, IIT considered this question as MARKS TO ALL in their final answer key.
